sentences.text: I just drank, thank you.

I don't know where I was-- they-- one time when we were in-- standing up in line in one morning in Augsburg, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was telling to my sister-- but not telling, just whispering very softly, I had to go to bathroom.
They give something, something they said coffee, something black to drink.
It was-- I don't know what was that.
But anyway, it was early morning.
It was cold before we went to the factory, maybe 5:00 o'clock 4:30 in the morning.
And we walked.
And we-- we walked out.
We were standing to have this liquid to drink.
And I said to my sister, Piri, I said, very, very softly, I have to go to bathroom.
And SS who was not even close by, came over there.
He hit me left and right.
You damn Jew!
You talking!
You should not open your mouth.
He was beating me up.
And I tell you, I was so scared.
I'm embarrassed to say, but I don't know if I should say or not.
I didn't have a panty.
But I peed on the-- you know.
I should say I peed in my panty.
But I didn't any anything, anything under.
I peed.
And I was beaten up for-- just because I whispered I had to go, had to, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And maybe 20 years ago, I would not tell this this way, because I'm old-fashioned.
I was always old-fashioned.
But, you know, in time, I think we should tell whatever happened, whatever remember.
I'm sure many more things happen.
But you can't remember everything.
I'm not-- write-- I didn't write down to prepare.
It's just asking, and whatever is comes to my mind, I tell you.
And another thing happened there.
In-- oh, yeah.
In meantime, I get sick.
And I had fever.
They wanted to take me to Dachau, but was not-- probably kill me.
But they could not take me because, again, close were-- either Russian or American, I don't know who were close.
So I was there.
And I was sick.
But I was working, still working.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we had lice all over.
All the clothes was lice.
You know, we could not shower every day or clean ourselves every day.
Anyway, I was working every day in a factory.
And they taught me each machine.
If a German-- because German people were there too.
We could not talk to anybody.
If a German didn't show up, the [NON-ENGLISH] the foreman showed me each machine.
He said, if you are going to do a, a sabotage, I want to tell you something, you'll be dead.
We'll kill you.
So I, I was too anxious to be-- not to killed because mostly I was not even worried about myself.
Because I said, I don't think I can take it too much longer.
But my sister was there.
And I had to live for her.
And I did all the machine.
Whatever they want, I, I worked on it.
And I was very, very fast, unbelievable.
They were showing off with me, the little one.
The [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they called me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Or [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], do you know what does it mean, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]?
How do you say it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]?
The youngster.
The youngster.
Or [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the little one.
Show to the other German how fast I am doing.
And one day, a Sunday-- Sunday we did not work.
We cleaned up over there, and did other work at the-- at the room.
And one day, SS came in.
And said, we need five people, six, I don't know how many people.
We are going to have kohlrabi.
Do you know what is kohlrabi.
Kohlrabi looks like a turnip.
But it's not turnip, light green, and has those leaves.
But in Hungary, what we did, we also cooked it in soup.
Or we could peel it and eat it raw.
And I said to my sister, I go because, you know, if we get a few kohlrabi, we won't be so hungry.
We can eat something.
He said, please.
She started crying.
She said, don't go.
Don't go, because don't you understand?
They will kill you.
This is just a story.
I said, Piri, I'm going to take a chance.
Maybe it's not a story.
I go.
I went.
I went down in basement.
And it was really-- we had to select the kohlrabi.
What happened, I had an old-- an old coat.
And the coat-- the coat had-- how you call it?
In-- lining, OK.
In the pocket, I open the pocket in my hand, torn off the pocket.
And the lining was all sewed.
And I filled the kohlrabi all over.
When we walked-- can I get up for a second?
No?
When I get up, I could hardly walk because it was all kohlrabi.
And we were supposed to get, I think, two kohlrabi-- who worked all Sunday there, one Sunday when we could have rest.
And I went.
And we had went up to the stairs.
This is like a building.
And the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] were there.
He's the one who was the head over there.
And looked at me, and looked at everybody, nobody did this.
And probably if I was not so young, I would not dare do it either, those crazy things.
And I could hardly walk.
But I walked up to the stairs.
He looked at, looked everybody.
He looked at me.
And I never forget.
He smiled.
Would you believe it?
Not really smiled, under the nose, he-- he did something like that.
And he give me two kohlrabi.
I think he felt sorry for him-- for me.
And when I went in, all the girls came over.
I don't think I had too much kohlrabi because everybody who get hand on it had the kohlrabi.
So I share that.
I shared with my sister and with, with, with friends.
Yeah.
So they did not kill me this time.
And they didn't kill me.
I mean, they killed many ways.
Me killed, you know.
Seven months, seven months.
And from there, they couldn't take me to the-- to Dachau.
They took me-- they took us-- they closed up the Messer-- they probably didn't close up the Messerschmitt.
They had to take us because it was closed to the American.
They took us to Muhldorf.
Muhldorf was-- they were-- they were working in-- oh, I have to tell you something else in, in Augsburg, a couple things.
One thing, I went to the bathroom.
Or-- you had to ask for bathroom.
They let you out if you ask, not every minute, but once a day, let's say.
I went out.
And I came-- when I coming back-- I'm embarrassed to say.
And that's why I said, I feel-- I understand people who get raped, you know, because not always their fault.
One man who was a older man-- older man, maybe 32 years old, I don't know.
To me, then, was older.
And he grabbed me, and grabbed me up front.
I didn't have nothing yet.
I was flat.
Not even 15, no, 14 and a half or something, yeah.
And, and started to grab me.
And I could not say anything, just [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That's all I asked.
Please don't do it.
Yeah, a German.
And, and look like he had a good time.
And I hardly could get out of it.
And I went back.
And you wouldn't believe it.
My sister still doesn't know that either.
I'm still embarrassed to tell, like it would be my fault.
That's why I was thinking about those people who are raped.
Some people may be asking for that.
But believe me, I would not ask something like that.
And I said again, dear God, just one more day.
Maybe you can save me, save us one more day.
Maybe two more, we'll be-- we'll be free.
That's how I, I went every day.
Every day, I prayed.
And every day, I said, just one more day.
Give us one more day.
And my sister was asking, what's happened?
I said, nothing.
I just went to the bathroom.
It's-- everything OK?
Everything OK.
Now, he didn't actually rape you, did he?
He did not.
He couldn't, because it was in the open.
If he did that, he would be in, in concentration camp.
When he saw somebody come-- coming, he had to let me go.
Otherwise, he would be in trouble, because I was a Jew.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
What else I wanted to ask about-- or tell about Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't know.
Did you ask me something?
Maybe I forgot.
After you finished working for the Messerschmitt, they didn't take you to Dachau, where did they take you?
Muhldorf.
Muhldorf, yes.
Excuse me.
I'm sorry.
I want to tell you one more thing in Auschwitz I just remembered.
They were bombing, bomb, in this, this factory.
In Auschwitz-- not Auschwitz, I'm sorry, in Muhldorf--
Augsburg.
Augsburg, Augsburg.
And they-- the, the halflings, the Jewish people, had a place to go.
It's like, like a basement where it was all over pipes-- you know, water pipes and things like that.
And this was the unsafest place.
And the SS didn't come in.
They locked us in.
And they were out somewhere else, so-called a safe place.
And we heard bombing.
And what happened-- or later they opened the door.
Before they opened the door, my sister was crying.
Said, Shari, we'll never get out of here.
We are going to-- we are going to be killed.
And this is-- that is the worst killing, because we are going to suffocated here.
And I was so afraid.
I said, Piri, don't worry.
We are not going to be killed.
God is with us.
You see we are going to be out from here.
And about half an hour later, everybody was panicking.
I was panicking inside.
But I didn't show it.
So we get out half an hour later.
And I don't know how many SS was dead.
Where they were, the safe place.
Would you believe it?
From there, they took-- I want to ask you something.
My sister wrote a poem.
Is that-- would you like to see it?
Yes, at the end of the segment.
We can read it at the end.
Certainly.
OK, please remind me.
And then from there, we went to Muhldorf.
And Muhldorf, we were there maybe a week or so-- few days, not-- two weeks.
And I was very sick then already.
Tuberculosis then, I already had tuberculosis, I think.
Pem-- pem-- there is another word, where what I had-- I had with the chest first problem, but not too bad.
But later, it became tuberculosis.
Maybe my son would able to tell you.
And then you can add that to it.
And we went back.
Again, was one-- how you call it?
A bucket-- they  give a bucket.
Men, women was in one, one wagon.
And was hardly any-- you could not even breathe.
And you were terribly thirsty-- hungry, yes, and thirsty, you, you think that's, that's, that's end.
You cannot be able to take it.
And there was one bucket.
And I had to go out.
I had to go out.
And I just couldn't, because another young boy was standing next to me, and other boys, and other men, and other women.
I just couldn't.
I had terrible pain in my stomach.
And I just couldn't do it.
Too embarrassed.
And they opened the door.
They open-- they took out the bucket.
And we saw some water, but was dirty water.
Very dirty, was like black water.
And we asked the SS, can we drink it?
No, because you would die from that.
And I believe it was poisoned water.
But we would even drink those, we were so thirsty.
So, finally, we, we, we arrive to Muhldorf.
And my sister went out.
They didn't even take me out anymore, because I couldn't hardly stand on my feet.
And they took out for the-- for the field.
And one day, he came-- she came home.
She said, no, this time I brought you something.
She get a potato, a raw potatoes.
And she said, you have to eat.
You, you have to get well, because how, you know, without you, I won't able to make it.
And she give me the raw potato.
And she was telling me.
I said, I was so worried.
I heard they were bombing the field.
She said, yes, and you know what?
The [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] he lost both of his legs.
And nothing happened to the Jewish people there.
Nothing happened.
And some German was also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] killed, maybe, but, but lost their-- part of their body.
Yeah.
And from there, they wanted to take me away.
And it was-- when they want to put me on the truck, it was too many people on the truck.
I just fell off.
This was mostly dead people, or dying people, like me.
To, to put us somewhere in the-- in the-- to dump us somewhere-- to bury us alive, dead or alive.
And I fell off.
And I crawled back to the-- to the barrack.
And then we get, again, a few days later, they took us to another cattle cart.
And they were there.
And they were taking us.
And one day, they opened the, the cattle car.
And they said-- before, before they opened it, there was shooting.
And I, I heard that this, the army began shooting.
They didn't know this was the Jewish people there.
They thought this-- they shooting the Germans.
And some people were killed in the same cattle car where I was and my sister.
We were hugging each other.
And next to me and next to her was people bleeding.
They died.
And, and then later, they opened the door.
And they said, you are free.
And we are starting to run with a few other girls.
And even to somebody's stable, attic.
And we were so happy.
We are free.
And this was a little village.
And we went to the attic.
And maybe an hour, two hours later, the German came with they-- with their guns.
Every [NON-ENGLISH]-- every Jewish people should come back.
Every Jewish people.
We didn't-- We thought we are going to be quiet, not going to go back.
But they found us.
We had to go back.
And when we went back, we saw blood all over.
It was shooting just whom they can shoot.
There was a lot of people were shot there.
But we, again, we were saved.
We went back to the car-- cattle car.
And we were there.
And I think the next day we were-- we were going.
And we were liberated in Tutzing.
And Tutzing was close to Feldafing.
From Tutzing, they took us to Feldafing.
The American, the American, yes.
And in, in Tutzing, we were liberated.
And I went out.
And in-- I was cold.
But I was very sick.
And I saw a-- how you call that?
Is where you-- the, the water comes.
When, when you have in the field, this water.
No, no, no, no, it's--
A well, yes, a well.
That's it.
Thank you.
And I washed myself in the cold water.
And I felt much better because I was fulled with lice.
And and-- but I could hardly walk.
And when we walked, we don't-- we didn't see anything.
We could not walk straight.
There was nothing.
It was always either dead body or dying people.
And they were begging, please, help.
And we could not.
You could not even walk without stepping on somebody.
It was thousand and thousand, hundred of thousand people.
And in one more days, I feel I would've been dead too.
I could not take it any longer.
And from-- they took us to Feldafing.
And in Feldafing, they put me in a-- like a little hospital for a couple of days.
And I was very sick.
And from there, they took me to Gauting, and that's-- in a hospital.
And this was a Catholic hospital with a-- with an ambulance.
And this was a Catholic hospital.
And we had nuns for, for nurses.
And we had some Jewish doctors.
And we had some German doctors.
And we were separated, the womans or the girls-- the female were in one building.
And the men was the other building.
And that's when-- Shony.
I met Shony.
But not right away.
How did you meet him?
How I met Shony.
This, this was-- this was the best part of it.
First of all, when I went to, to the ambulance, I said to myself, I don't know what going to happen.
I, I don't think I'm going to live or die.
But I just one more times, I would like to see my mother and my grandfather.
And I know, thanks God, my sister is OK.
But I was going away from her too.
Not too far-- I don't know how far was Gauting and Feldafing.
But anyway, they took me over there.
And we were fed well in the-- in the hospital.
And one day, when my window was open, I heard somebody playing beautiful violin music.
And I said to my roommate, I said, who plays this beautiful violin music.
It's, it's, it's unbelievable, this music.
It's-- anyway, I love music.
But it was so beautiful.
I said, who plays this music, this-- must have a beautiful soul and heart.
And nobody can tell-- nobody could tell me who it was.
And one day, I looked out the window.
And I saw another boy-- a boy walking.
I said boy, because we were all young there.
And I said, [INAUDIBLE], I said, do you know who's playing the violin so beautiful?
He said, yes.
It's a dying boy in this building.
And this was his last wish, to get a violin from the-- he told to the doctor, his last wish would be if they would get a-- if he would get a violin.
And whenever he felt a little bit better, he was playing the violin.
And that-- this was a healing for me, I tell you.
And much later, I met Shony personally.
One time, he came to the hos-- to the room, was looking for his sister, if we saw his sister.
And I still didn't know this is Shony, who was the violinist.
And he was very polite, very nice, very refined person, very handsome.
And I said to girl-- my roommate-- we didn't see his-- we told him, we don't-- we didn't see-- we don't know his, his sister.
We never met her.
We never saw her.
And then he walked out.
And my, my roommate said to me, he's so nice guy, so handsome.
And he's so polite.
He didn't even talk to us, just want to ask for his sister.
We went-- went in the hallway after him.
And he disappeared.
He went another room, and asking for his sister.
So.
And I liked him.
And my, my roommate liked him.
And we still didn't know he was the violinist.
And one day, right-- after a few weeks after, I was standing in the-- at the window.
And the boys was going out.
And I saw this guy again.
I said-- oh.
I said-- we had tomatoes on the window, I think two tomatoes, belonged to my girlfriend, and-- or my roommate.
And she would not give it away, because tomatoes, it's nothing today.
But in those days, you could not buy for anything tomatoes.
And she get it from somebody.
And she look-- he looked up.
And he said, would you throw me a tomato?
I said, sure.
I throw the tomatoes.
And he said to me, even today, you caught me with the tomatoes.
And then I found out who he is.
And, I-- you know, and then started to go out together.
And it, it was very nice.
I think '49, end of '49.
But didn't go out with anybody else.
And he didn't go out with anybody else.
And we love each other.
At least, I love him.
[LAUGHTER]
I know, yeah.
And every night when we go to bed, I always said-- and he said the same thing-- good to be together.
Good to be together.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [CRYING] Anyway.
Yes, after we married, we came to the United States.
And it was very hard the first few months.
Was very hard, and few-- first few years.
Then it was very interesting.
It-- We had a very beautiful life, very colorful life.
You meet-- we met a lot of interesting people.
And Shony did work in the business, you know-- he-- and still working, thanks God.
He's a fine violinist and a composer.
And through his work, we, we met a lot of-- lot of people.
Not just show business people, but we met people other field.
We met Jonas Salk, you know.
And we met [INAUDIBLE] who was the right hand of, of Albert Einstein.
And we met, you know, a lot of scientists, and artists, and writers, and scholars.
And it's, it's been a very interesting life.
But you never forget.
You never forget what happened to you-- ever, ever.
Thanks God, we have two children.
And Dinah and Bobby.
Yeah, thanks God, wonderful grandchildren, yes.
My sister is in Agoura.
She lives in Agoura.
Not too far.
We talk every day.
And my brother is in Westwood.
Unfortunately very sick for two years.
This is a very interesting question.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First, when I was in Gauting, I was very, very much done.
I thought, I'm going to be a-- I am going to have a nervous breakdown, because I was waiting, waiting for my mother and my grandfather.
And I couldn't-- I couldn't find them.
So I was in very bad shape.
And this was before I met Shony, because he really-- he was healing.
He's healing on me.
His music is really a healing on me.
And one day, my, my sister came in.
She visited as much as she could.
She came in.
And she said to me, Shari, are you OK?
I said, yes.
She said, don't get excited.
And-- and don't-- God forbid, nothing-- I hope nothing will happen to you.
But it's, it's good news.
And opened the door.
And my brother came in.
I tell you.
I cried from happiness.
This my brother, who was-- who was like a father to me, and very brilliant man.
He was always a brilliant man.
He was a missile designer engineer for Douglas for about 30 years, so-- and a good person.
And he taught me to be decent, and honest, and good, just like my mother told me, and my grandfather.
And something I wanted to tell.
I-- I don't know what was it.
So he's right now very sick.
And it has effect on me.
I-- you know, two years, he's in bed.
And it's very hard, very hard.
But I have two wonderful children.
And I have the wonderful grandchildren.
And thanks God for them.
And thank God for my husband.
They keeping me alive.
Don't hate anybody.
Just, just don't hate anybody, because look what happened from, from the hatred.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because somebody has a different religion, or different race, or-- you shouldn't look at that.
You should look for the person, the human being, what is inside.
And if somebody a good human being, that's what you have to look.
And the life is not forever.
We are just here as a visitors.
So it's not worth it to hate and kill, because we are not going to be all of our time here.
Might as not be, be good to each other.
Thank you so very much.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Read the--
Yes, yes.
